Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered, which I'm not proud of. I tried to dedicate about 20 hours a week, but it wasn't consistent day-to-day. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes, which took longer than just reading. Some weeks I was on top of things, other weeks my anxiety got the better of me and I'd avoid studying altogether until panic set in.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on deadlines and honestly, on how scared I was feeling about the material. When anxiety was high, I'd either hyperfocus for days or completely shut down and avoid everything. I tried to set regular times on Sundays and Wednesdays, but life always seemed to throw something at me. Deadlines were really what dictated when I actually sat down to work.
Q3: Usually it was fear, honestly. I'd log in when I remembered an assignment was due or when that creeping dread reminded me I hadn't checked in for a few days. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or a peer message, and that would prompt me to log in. The peer support connections actually helped remind me to stay engaged when I was feeling overwhelmed.
Q4: There were a few weeks mid-semester where logging in became more automatic, usually after I'd connected with some peers who were also struggling. Having those three people I could message made the LMS feel less intimidating. But that routine fell apart when my anxiety spiked around exam time. It never became truly habitual for me—it always required conscious effort.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I'm such a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. The discussion forums were also helpful when peers explained things in different ways than the instructor. When I actually engaged with those resources, my understanding improved significantly, which showed in my assignment scores.
Q6: I avoided the reading-heavy PDFs and text documents a lot. They just didn't stick for me the way visual content did. I also minimally used the additional practice quizzes because they triggered my anxiety—seeing wrong answers made me spiral. I knew they'd probably help, but the fear of confirming I didn't understand something kept me away.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate results or when it connected to an upcoming assignment. If I could visualize how the content would help me on an exam or project, I'd engage with it. The peer support discussions felt valuable too because they made me feel less alone in struggling. Anything that reduced my anxiety rather than amplified it was worth doing.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps to me, honestly. Required ones I had to do regardless of how scared I was, so I'd push through. But optional ones? My brain would say 'you don't HAVE to feel anxious about this right now' and I'd skip them. It's frustrating because I know some of those optional resources could have helped me, but without the pressure of a grade, my avoidance won.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything and studied from printed materials and my own handwritten notes constantly. I'd watch a video once online, then work from my notes for hours afterward. I also used YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't understand, and I'd study with one of my peer support partners over coffee, which obviously didn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: So many times. I'd download a lecture, watch it once in the LMS, then replay my downloaded version three or four more times while making visual study guides. I'd spend hours with my colored markers creating diagrams on paper. My best studying happened with my notes spread across my bed, completely offline. The LMS would just show that one initial video view.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a PDF or video once, download it immediately, and then that was my primary study material for days. My laptop has folders full of course content that I reviewed repeatedly offline. It was actually less anxiety-inducing to study from downloaded materials because I wasn't constantly seeing my incomplete task list or upcoming deadlines on the LMS.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart for me. My anxiety was through the roof, and I started avoiding the course entirely for about a week. I'd open the LMS, see all the things I was behind on, feel paralyzed with fear, and close it. The peer support intervention actually helped pull me back—having someone check in on me made me feel accountable in a supportive way rather than a scary way.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor, more than workload or confusion. When I felt overwhelmed, I'd log in less because seeing my incomplete tasks made me feel worse. Fatigue from poor sleep due to worry also affected me. I had the resources and time, technically, but my mental state determined whether I could actually use them productively.
Q14: Many times. I'd log in, click through materials, watch videos while my mind was racing about everything I was behind on. I'd be physically present in the LMS but mentally just going through motions. Sometimes I'd open discussion posts and read them without really processing anything because I was too scared about upcoming deadlines to focus.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important. The fear of doing them poorly was stronger than the fear of not doing them at all, which makes no logical sense but that's anxiety for you. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel just as scared. My completion rate suffered because of this avoidance pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my only motivator. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then panic-log in the night before something was due. My activity probably looked very spiky—nothing, nothing, nothing, then frantic engagement. The fear of missing a deadline was the only thing stronger than my fear of engaging with the material.
Q17: Good feedback helped a lot—seeing that I'd done well on an assignment (like my 83% average) gave me courage to engage more. But waiting for feedback was torture. I'd avoid logging in because I was scared to see my grades. The peer support helped here too; my study partners would encourage me to check feedback together so I wasn't facing it alone.
Q18: The course structure with 18 different online modules felt overwhelming to me. Having so many pieces made it hard to see the big picture, which increased my anxiety. I appreciated when things were broken into clear weeks, but the sheer volume made me feel perpetually behind. A more streamlined structure might have helped me stay engaged.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched my learning style. But the discussion requirements were hard—my anxiety about saying something wrong in front of peers made me post minimally. The flexibility of online access was both good and bad; good because I could study when calm, bad because I could easily avoid everything when scared.
Q20: I think that's exactly what happens when someone is anxious and trying to figure things out but can't bring themselves to submit. You're in the LMS reading everything, watching videos, trying to understand, but the fear of submitting imperfect work stops you from actually turning anything in. High activity doesn't mean high confidence or completion.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I performed reasonably well—got a Merit—despite moderate LMS activity because so much of my real learning happened offline with my visual notes and downloaded materials. Someone could study effectively outside the system and only log in to submit work. The LMS doesn't capture studying, just clicking.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked, but they completely miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if you were anxiously scrolling or deeply learning. They miss offline studying entirely, they miss the emotional state you were in, and they definitely miss peer support that happens outside the platform, like my coffee study sessions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious and want to learn more, when you think about course concepts outside of study time. For me, engagement also means feeling safe enough to participate despite anxiety. It's mental and emotional investment, not just time logged.
Q24: The LMS would never show how scared I was most of the time, or how much courage it took just to log in some days. It wouldn't show my peer support partner texting me encouragement, or the hours I spent with printed notes trying to make sense of things. It wouldn't show that I actually cared deeply about this course even when my behavior looked disengaged. My fear and my effort existed simultaneously, but only one is visible in the data.
